stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Quantifying and detailing the "chilling effect" on U.S. citizen children; harm to specific, vulnerable lawfully present populations (DACA, TPS); exacerbating existing racial and economic inequities; and systemic discrimination.
specificPoints: They provide data-driven legal and policy analysis of the consequences. They consistently cite studies from the Urban Institute and KFF to prove the chilling effect is real and predictable, provide demographic data on the millions of children in mixed-status families, and detail the specific categories of lawfully present immigrants who would be harmed.
Rescind the rule and invest in outreach to rebuild trust with immigrant communities.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Education Stakeholders (Head Start Directors, Teachers, Associations)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Direct harm to child development; exclusion of the most vulnerable children from life-changing services; destruction of decades of trust built with immigrant communities.
specificPoints: This group frames the issue as "punishing children for circumstances beyond their control." They uniquely describe how the fear created by the rule will destabilize classrooms and program funding by causing enrollment drops among both eligible and ineligible families, turning schools into perceived "ICE traps."
Immediately rescind the reinterpretation and reaffirm that Head Start is not a "federal public benefit" subject to these restrictions.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Government Entities (Members of Congress, City/County Offices, Health Departments)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Shifting the burden of care and cost to local governments; undermining local public health initiatives; creating fear and distrust that makes all public service delivery more difficult; contradiction of legislative intent.
specificPoints: They focus on the operational disruption to local government and the conflict between federal policy and local responsibilities. They frame the rule as an unfunded mandate that will increase costs for local taxpayers by pushing patients into emergency rooms.
Withdraw the interpretation to avoid harming local communities and their service infrastructure.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Fear's Tangible Consequences: A social worker provides a powerful and specific account of the "chilling effect," moving beyond abstraction to list concrete outcomes she has witnessed: "They live in housing so substandard it is not legal. They don't have food. They are exploited by employers. Abused women stay with their abusers."

Abuse and Exploitation Leverage: The Alliance for Immigrant Survivors offers a chilling insight: the rule is not just a barrier to care, but a tool for perpetrators. They argue the restrictions will give "leverage to abusers and perpetrators of crime to use threats and intimidation" against immigrant survivors, knowing their victims have fewer avenues for support.

Generational Impact of Access: An individual provides a powerful personal narrative of the "three-generational impact" of Head Start. Their grandmother, an immigrant from Mexico, was supported by the program, which enabled her children to thrive, who then supported the commenter, illustrating the long-term societal return on investment in immigrant families.

Data-Backed Proof of the "Chilling Effect": Multiple advocacy groups cited a specific, powerful statistic: from 2016-2019, participation in Medicaid, CHIP, and SNAP among citizen children with noncitizen family members "fell twice as fast" as among those in all-citizen households, providing quantitative evidence for their primary concern.

Data-Backed Proof of the "Chilling Effect": Multiple advocacy groups cited a specific, powerful statistic: from 2016-2019, participation in Medicaid, CHIP, and SNAP among citizen children with noncitizen family members "fell twice as fast" as among those in all-citizen households, providing quantitative evidence for their primary concern.

Data-Backed Proof of the "Chilling Effect": Multiple advocacy groups cited a specific, powerful statistic: from 2016-2019, participation in Medicaid, CHIP, and SNAP among citizen children with noncitizen family members "fell twice as fast" as among those in all-citizen households, providing quantitative evidence for their primary concern.

A Non-Partisan Fiscal Argument Against the Rule: A self-described "lifelong Republican" and long-time FQHC board member opposes the rule, arguing that excluding immigrants from preventative care is fiscally irresponsible because it forces more expensive emergency care later.

Forgotten Population: Foster Youth: John Burton Advocates for Youth points out the rule would harm foster youth with uncertain immigration status, who "did not choose to immigrate... nor did they choose to be the victim of abuse and neglect," yet would be denied essential support.

The Workforce Is Also Immigrant: A few commenters make the unique point that Head Start programs and the broader healthcare workforce rely on immigrant educators and workers, suggesting the rule could harm not only the recipients of services but also the providers themselves.

The Workforce Is Also Immigrant: A few commenters make the unique point that Head Start programs and the broader healthcare workforce rely on immigrant educators and workers, suggesting the rule could harm not only the recipients of services but also the providers themselves.

Unique Vulnerability of Pacific Islands: The Pacific Islands Primary Care Association points out that the rule would have "immediate and severe effects" in U.S.-Affiliated Pacific Islands, where CHCs are the primary health system for entire communities with large populations of lawfully present non-citizens who would be rendered ineligible.

Unexpected Connection to Abuse Cycles: REACH, a service provider for victims of sexual violence, argues that Head Start is a vital tool for disrupting intergenerational cycles of abuse and trafficking, and that barring immigrant children makes them more vulnerable.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Unintended Consequences as a Central Theme: The "chilling effect" is the most dominant pattern. Commenters consistently argue the rule's impact will spread far beyond its intended targets, creating a shadow public health crisis among eligible citizens and lawfully present immigrants who become too afraid to seek help. Another recurring pattern is the prediction that denying low-cost primary care will inevitably shift costs to high-cost emergency care, increasing the overall burden on the system.

Experience Correlations: There is a stark and consistent pattern based on professional role. Every single commenter identifying as a frontline worker—physician, nurse, social worker, educator, FQHC staffer—passionately opposed the rule, citing direct experience with the populations it would harm and the ethical conflicts it would create.

Erosion of Trust as a Systemic Threat: Multiple commenters from different sectors identify the destruction of trust as a primary, long-lasting harm. They argue that once immigrant communities perceive clinics and schools as arms of immigration enforcement, that trust will be "irreparably harmed" and will undermine public health and social service engagement for years to come.

Facility-type Patterns: Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs) and other safety-net providers are unanimous in their opposition. They consistently argue the rule is an existential threat that conflicts directly with their legal mandate, operational model, and mission to serve entire communities.

Geographic Variations: While opposition is national, comments from states with large immigrant populations like California, Texas, New York, and Florida provide specific data on the scale of the impact, grounding the national debate in local reality.

keyQuotations.quote: The chilling effect of this HHS notice cannot be overstated. Research shows that removing eligibility for immigrant communities harms even those who remain eligible, including U.S. citizens and lawful permanent residents, who will avoid health care for fear that doing so might jeopardize a family member’s immigration status.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Association of Asian Pacific Community Health Organizations

keyQuotations.quote: From 2016–2019, participation in Medicaid, CHIP, and SNAP among citizen children with noncitizen household members declined twice as fast as participation among those in all-citizen households due to fear and confusion over immigration policy changes.

If a parent or guardian feels threatened taking their child to the doctor, those children could lose access to vital health care if the alternative is family separation, economic ruin, trauma, or worse.

This shift is not only a direct attack on immigrant families that opens the door to racial profiling, but also risks devastating Head Start programs, and the communities they serve, across the country.
It is ridiculous to deny children, CHILDREN, the benefit of this program based on the simple fact that their parents are undocumented.
My parents will definitely be affected, they have so many chronic conditions and take many medications that will be not able to pay... This will definitely be devastating for all of us.
People will die if public health services are kept from immigrants.
It breaks my heart to know that I am NOW being asked to turn away patients due to their legal status.
Designating the Health Center Program as federal public benefit would directly conflict with this statutory directive, forcing health centers to violate the law by turning patients away...
When medical providers must require proof of immigration status, we don’t just push one group into the shadows; we undermine the health of entire communities.
Even if you don't care about the health and well-being of immigrants, you likely don't want your family to get multidrug resistant tuberculosis.
Excluding children from education because of their parents’ citizenship status is unconstitutional and purely evil.
Denying young children access to education, food and a stable school environment, because their parents may be undocumented, is morally repugnant.
To discriminate based on one's immigration status is racist, classist, and a clear violation of human rights.
I hope you never have to feel the terror of being undocumented in a country that hates you.
People do not flee violence, famine, or extreme poverty because they expect to get a primary care appointment. They flee because they have no other choice.

I have seen the horrors my immigrant patients have endured in their home countries and I do not believe this direct will dissuade them from coming to or remaining in the US but it will make all of us sicker.
Finally! Our government is protecting those of us that pay taxes and work hard but removing free care to people that are not even US citizens.
I demand that no undocumented children should be able to access those programs or our public school system whatsoever. Deport them all.
I do not agree that illegal immigrants are viable for this Countries benefits of any kind.

sections.executiveSummary:
Public sentiment is overwhelmingly opposed to the policy, with a near-unanimous consensus across hundreds of comments that it will cause significant, direct, and foreseeable harm to U.S. citizen children. The dominant concern, articulated by nearly every stakeholder group, is that the policy will create a powerful "chilling effect" where fear, confusion, and distrust deter immigrant parents from enrolling their legally eligible U.S. citizen children in essential health, nutrition, and early education programs.

Commenters further argue that this chilling effect will lead to the destabilization and potential collapse of community-based services like Head Start and Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs), as reduced enrollment triggers funding cuts that harm all families in a community, regardless of immigration status. A core tension exists between this widespread, evidence-based view and a very small minority of commenters who believe the policy is necessary to preserve scarce resources for citizens. The single most dominant recommendation from the public is the immediate and complete withdrawal of the proposed policy.

consensusPoints.text: A "chilling effect" will deter eligible families from accessing services. Commenters across all stakeholder groups agree that fear of immigration enforcement, confusion over complex eligibility rules, and general distrust of government will cause parents in mixed-status families to forgo or withdraw their legally-entitled U.S. citizen children from vital programs like Medicaid, CHIP, Head Start, and community health services. Many note this is a predictable, documented phenomenon, not speculation.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly every commenter who opposed the policy (over 98% of all commenters)

consensusPoints.text: Program instability will harm all children in a community. Commenters, including thousands who submitted form letters, argue that the policy will harm citizen children by destabilizing the programs they rely on. They predict that reduced enrollment from immigrant families will lead to funding cuts and potential closures for Head Start centers and community clinics, diminishing access and quality for all families in that community, regardless of immigration status.

The policy unjustly penalizes U.S. citizen children for their parents' status. Commenters framed the issue in moral, ethical, and legal terms. They argue it is fundamentally unfair and potentially unconstitutional to deny a U.S. citizen child access to services they are legally entitled to because of their parents' immigration status—a circumstance over which the child has no control. Many legal experts and advocacy groups argue that by defining eligibility based on a household "unit," the policy would directly deny services to U.S. citizen children.

A large population of U.S. citizen children is at risk. Many advocacy groups, healthcare providers, and policy experts repeatedly cite the statistic that one in four children in the U.S. has at least one immigrant parent, and the vast majority of these children are U.S. citizens. This point is used to frame the potential harm as a widespread national problem affecting millions of American children, not a niche issue.

areasOfDebate.topic: Overall Impact on U.S. Citizen Children
areasOfDebate.description: The public input reveals not a balanced debate but a near-unanimous position met with a very small, starkly contrasting perspective.

positions.label: Widespread Indirect Harm
positions.stance: The policy will cause significant harm to U.S. citizen children by creating fear that prevents their parents from accessing services, destabilizing community programs, and unjustly punishing children for their parents' status.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by over 98% of unique perspectives across all batches, including nearly all organizations, healthcare providers, government entities, and the vast majority of individuals.
keyArguments: The "chilling effect" is a proven phenomenon that deters even eligible families, as evidenced by disproportionate enrollment drops from 2016-2019.
Services like Head Start and community health centers operate on a community-wide basis; harming access for one group degrades the service and infrastructure for all, including citizens.
A U.S. citizen child's rights and legal entitlements should not be contingent on the status of their parents.
New administrative burdens for verification will create barriers and delays for all vulnerable families, especially citizens who may struggle with documentation.

positions.label: Resource Preservation / Prioritization of Citizens
positions.stance: The policy will benefit U.S. citizen children by ensuring that limited federal resources are reserved for them and are not being used by non-citizens.
positions.supportLevel: A small handful of individual commenters across all batches.
keyArguments: Taxpayer-funded benefits should be reserved for vulnerable Americans first.
Programs like Head Start are already oversubscribed with long waitlists, meaning citizens are being denied spots.
The rule does not change the eligibility of U.S. citizens, who can still receive benefits if they apply.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The systemic and evidence-based "chilling effect"; the direct denial of entitled benefits to citizen children in mixed-status families; deepening racial and economic disparities; and the long-term societal costs of disinvestment in child health and education.
specificPoints: These groups consistently provided robust data and legal analysis. They frequently cited studies on benefit disenrollment rates among citizen children in mixed-status families between 2016-2019 as empirical proof that chilling effects are real and predictable. They frame the issue as a violation of children's rights and a threat to national well-being.
Complete and immediate withdrawal of the policy.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers & Organizations (FQHCs, Hospitals, Pediatricians, Associations)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The erosion of patient trust; immediate public health risks from citizen children missing vaccinations and preventive care; the operational strain on safety-net facilities; and the violation of their mandate to serve all residents.
specificPoints: They offer frontline, clinical accounts and emphasize the downstream effects on the entire healthcare system. They predict that reduced access for some will lead to longer wait times for all patients, increased uncompensated care in emergency rooms, and higher costs for insured citizens.
Rescind the policy to protect the statutory mandate of Community Health Centers and prevent a public health crisis.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Education & Child Welfare Stakeholders (Head Start, School Districts, etc.)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Program destabilization through enrollment drops and subsequent funding cuts; the long-term negative educational outcomes for children denied early learning; and new administrative burdens diverting resources from teaching.
specificPoints: They focus on the direct, practical harm to the operational viability of their programs and highlight the knock-on effects for the K-12 education system. They argue that citizen children will be harmed when their unprepared classmates (who missed Head Start) require more classroom resources, undermining learning for everyone.
Withdraw the policy to prevent program closures and maintain the long-standing interpretation that Head Start is not a "federal public benefit" subject to these restrictions.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Government Entities (City/County Governments, State Agencies, School Districts)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Shifting costs to local taxpayers; increased administrative burden on public agencies; legal conflicts with state law; and negative downstream consequences like increased foster care placements and emergency room usage.
specificPoints: They highlight the fiscal, operational, and intergovernmental friction the policy creates, arguing it imposes unfunded mandates and will lead to service delays and barriers for all residents, not just immigrants.
Reversal of the policy to avoid imposing unfunded mandates and negative social outcomes on local communities.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Data-Backed Observation on Disenrollment: The most powerful insight, repeated by dozens of advocacy groups and experts, is the empirical data from 2016-2019 showing that similar immigration-related policies had a disproportionately negative "chilling effect" on the program participation of U.S. citizen children in mixed-status families. This transforms the concern from a prediction into a documented, measurable outcome. (Cited by Children's HealthWatch, SEIU, APHA, and many others).

Unexpected Connection to Foster Care: The NY Department of State's Office for New Americans made a powerful and unique argument that restricting KinGAP for undocumented relatives would sever family ties for U.S. citizen children in crisis, likely forcing them into the more expensive and traumatic state foster care system instead of being cared for by kin.

Harm to the K-12 Classroom Environment: Several commenters, including an academic and a Montessori provider, argued that the policy harms U.S. citizen children academically. When immigrant children are excluded from Head Start and arrive at kindergarten unprepared, it negatively impacts the entire classroom dynamic and can lower the academic performance of their U.S.-born peers.

Compelling Personal Example of the "Chilling Effect": An FQHC policy expert provided a stark, real-world case: a legal permanent resident mother, out of fear of the policy environment, pulled her three U.S. citizen daughters from Medicaid and canceled necessary specialty medical care. This single story makes the abstract concept of a "chilling effect" devastatingly concrete.

Fiscal Contribution Insight: End Child Poverty California pointed out that "U.S. citizen children born to first-generation immigrants are among the strongest fiscal and economic contributors in the country," arguing that harming their development is not only unjust but also economically counterproductive for the nation.

Bureaucracy as a Barrier for All Vulnerable Citizens: Several commenters pointed out that new documentation requirements would create unintended barriers for U.S. citizens who struggle to obtain records, such as children experiencing homelessness, those in the foster care or kinship care systems, or those in rural areas.

Compelling Personal Example of Unintended Consequences: Latino Network illustrated a severe outcome with the scenario of a mother dying in childbirth. Under the new rule, the grieving father could be denied access to health programs for his now motherless, U.S. citizen infant, a time when "his family needed support more than ever."

emergingPatterns.pattern: Unintended Consequences as the Central Theme: The dominant pattern across all batches is the argument that the policy's intended target (non-citizens) is vastly overshadowed by its severe, foreseeable, and harmful collateral damage to U.S. citizens. Commenters repeatedly traced a chain reaction of harm from individual disenrollment to community-wide service collapse.

Broad, Cross-Sectoral Opposition: The analysis reveals an exceptionally broad coalition opposing the policy, uniting labor unions (SEIU, AFL-CIO), civil rights organizations (MALDEF, ACLU), healthcare providers (APHA, AAP), child welfare advocates (Save the Children), faith groups (USCCB), and local/state governments. This deep alignment underscores the perceived severity of the threat.

Experience Correlation: A clear pattern emerged where commenters with direct service delivery experience (e.g., pediatricians, Head Start directors, FQHC staff) provided the most granular and visceral examples of harm, while advocacy and legal groups provided the statistical and legal architecture that validated these frontline accounts.

Moral and Ethical Framing: Beyond practical arguments, a strong pattern of moral framing was evident. Commenters repeatedly used words like "cruel," "immoral," "inhumane," and "un-American" to describe punishing children for their parents' status, arguing it violates core national values.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] fallout will impact funding streams for all children, not just the ones intended to target.
[The] predictable consequences would harm all Head Start families, citizens, and noncitizens alike.
[The trauma of family separation could] cause irreparable mental health trauma on the country’s future adult work force.
Banning children from accessing essential services based on the status and decisions of their parents is cruel and unnecessary.
Confusion about eligibility and fear of immigration consequences may discourage even eligible individuals, including U.S. citizen children, from accessing needed care.

Data show that in 2016-2019, participation among US citizen children in mixed-status families in programs like Medicaid, CHIP, and SNAP decreased nearly twice as fast as children with US citizen parents due to feelings of fear and uncertainty caused by changes to immigration policy.
Does it make things better for U.S. born children if their classmates who are immigrants come to school less prepared? No, any teacher will tell you that it makes things worse.
Her being afraid to access health care at a FQHC will have long term health implications for this child and in the long run more expensive treatment.
I'M TALKING FOR MY CHILDREN, THEY DON'T HAVE A VOICE, I' M THEIR VOICES!!!! They were born in U.S.A.
In the current climate of fear, many households will adopt a ‘better safe than sorry’ approach, and American citizens will suffer as a result.
My daughter thrived in her Head Start and I could not have cared less about the citizenship of her classmates.

Nearly one in four children in the U.S. has at least one immigrant parent, and the vast majority of these children are U.S. citizens. By restricting access to foundational programs, this rule punishes citizen children for their parents’ immigration status...
Refusing health care to people based on their immigration status will degrade the health of our entire community, including US citizens.
The children affected are often U.S. citizens who will now suffer from policies targeting their family members.
The health of my pediatric patients depends on their parents and caregivers having access to care.
To take a spot from legal residents who live in a more at-risk environment (for educational deficits) is unconscionable.

Under the guise of excluding immigrants from services, HHS’s re-interpretation will harm the health and well-being of all Americans by purporting to require grantees to divert program funds to impose a 'show me your papers' verification regime...
When we cut down the educational advantages of one, we do it to all children.
Why should U.S. citizen children be denied access to programs that they have a right to as citizens of this country?
This change is necessary for American children’s health... to ensure there is money to help babies, families, and the future of our country... instead of wasting our resources on illegal aliens.

9.3.themeDescription: Harm to Vulnerable U.S. Citizens Lacking Documentation

sections.executiveSummary: There is universal and emphatic agreement among commenters that the policy will unintentionally harm a wide range of vulnerable U.S. citizens by creating insurmountable documentation barriers to essential services. The primary tension is not whether this harm will occur—all agree it will—but whether the barriers are primarily practical hurdles (cost, time) or reflections of deeper systemic discrimination. The overwhelming recommendation from all stakeholder groups is to rescind the reinterpretation and revert to previous guidance to prevent the denial of care and support to the very populations—the unhoused, children in foster care, disaster survivors, and other marginalized citizens—that safety-net programs are designed to serve.

consensusPoints.text: The policy will create insurmountable barriers for vulnerable U.S. citizens. Virtually all 177 unique perspectives agree that requiring proof of citizenship or legal status will inadvertently block access to services for many eligible U.S. citizens who cannot easily produce required documents. This is seen not as a minor inconvenience but as an effective denial of service.

exceptions.text: None. This point represents a complete consensus across all stakeholder types.

consensusPoints.text: The process of obtaining documents is costly, time-consuming, and complex. Commenters, particularly those from Head Start programs and direct service providers, detail the significant logistical and financial hurdles involved in acquiring or replacing official documents like birth certificates. They argue the policy overlooks the "time poverty" and financial constraints of low-income families.

exceptions.text: None.

consensusPoints.text: Specific populations are at extreme and predictable risk of harm. Commenters identify a consistent list of U.S. citizen populations who are most likely to be harmed due to their life circumstances making it difficult to secure and maintain documentation. The most frequently cited at-risk groups are: individuals experiencing homelessness, children in foster care, survivors of natural disasters, survivors of domestic violence, residents of rural areas, the elderly, Native Americans and Alaska Natives (AI/AN), and individuals with disabilities or severe mental illness.

exceptions.text: None.

areasOfDebate.description: While there is no debate that the policy will harm vulnerable citizens, commenters frame the nature of the problem in two distinct ways.

positions.label: Practical Hurdles
positions.stance: The primary problem is that documentation requirements are logistically difficult, expensive, and time-consuming for people in crisis or poverty.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters, especially direct service providers like Head Start programs and FQHCs.
keyArguments: Lost documents during moves or disasters are common
families lack funds to pay for birth certificates or transportation to government offices
a single missing record can derail enrollment during a critical developmental window

positions.label: Systemic Discrimination
positions.stance: The documentation barriers are not just practical but are rooted in and exacerbate historical and ongoing systemic discrimination.
positions.supportLevel: A significant minority, particularly national advocacy groups focused on civil rights and health equity.
keyArguments: Documentation challenges disproportionately affect people of color
historical injustices (e.g., Jim Crow era) resulted in a lack of official birth records for many African American elders
barriers for Native Americans are linked to sovereignty and historical policy failures

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Enrollment delays for at-risk children missing critical developmental windows; the high cost and time burden on already-stressed families; the erosion of trust built over decades with vulnerable communities.
specificPoints: They are already on the front lines of helping families navigate complex documentation issues (e.g., for proof of age or income) and have firsthand knowledge of how difficult and time-consuming this already is, even without citizenship verification.
They propose to maintain the status quo where Head Start is not considered a "Federal public benefit" subject to these verification rules.
The Sequatchie Valley Head Start program notes that approximately 30% of their children are raised by grandparents or other relatives who may not have the child's birth certificate.
A Head Start Director reports it can take "months to years" for a family to obtain a birth certificate, even with the program's active assistance.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Violating their statutory mandate to serve all residents; being forced to turn away patients in crisis; the "chilling effect" discouraging even eligible citizens from seeking care; potential legal liability for patient abandonment.
specificPoints: They emphasize that health crises (mental health emergencies, substance use treatment, unexpected illness) are precisely the moments when a person is least likely to have their documents in order, making a verification requirement operationally and ethically impossible.
They propose to explicitly exempt health centers and other crisis services from the verification requirement.

A psychiatrist at a Community Health Center for the homeless states, "Among the powerful strengths of the Community Health Center model is that we will give you access to vital, life saving care when you need it, even if you aren't able to show us your birth certificate".
Famcare Inc. recounts providing care to a young woman who had escaped human trafficking and had no ID.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Exacerbating systemic inequities; the disproportionate impact on communities of color, people with disabilities, and other marginalized groups; the violation of civil rights.
specificPoints: These groups often bring in external data to quantify the scale of the problem and place it within a broader context of social and racial justice. They highlight that documentation barriers are often a direct result of past and present discriminatory policies.
They propose a complete rescission of the policy reinterpretation.
The Washington Association for Community Health and others cite a 2024 national survey finding that 21.3 million American citizens lack ready access to documents proving citizenship.
The Robert Wood Johnson Foundation connects the lack of birth certificates for some African American elders to "systemic discrimination during the Jim Crow era".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The infeasibility of implementing a verification regime without clear guidance or funding; shifting healthcare and social service costs to local taxpayers; undermining local public health and safety efforts.
specificPoints: As the entities that would be responsible for implementation, they focus on the operational chaos, unfunded mandates, and legal contradictions the policy would create, framing it as an unworkable "show me your papers" regime.
They propose to revert to the previous, long-standing guidance.
A letter from 21 Members of Congress asks policymakers to "Imagine being required to bring a birth certificate to get care at a Community Health Center".
A coalition of major cities and counties state the policy will "place essential human services out of the reach of many vulnerable citizens".

noteworthyInsights.insight: A foster parent detailed the immense bureaucratic struggle to obtain a birth certificate for their 17-year-old U.S.-born foster son, whose parents were also U.S.-born. They concluded, "he easily could have left our house at age 18 without the documentation that would allow him to seek health care in this new interpretation of PRWORA," powerfully illustrating how even "straightforward" cases can be incredibly complex.

Multiple commenters cited survey data indicating that over 21 million U.S. citizens (roughly 9-10%) lack ready access to documents proving their citizenship, providing a quantitative scale to the potential problem.

The Robert Wood Johnson Foundation provided a sharp historical insight, noting that some "African American elders... lack official birth documentation as a direct consequence of systemic discrimination during the Jim Crow era and beyond." This reframes the issue from a simple bureaucratic hurdle to one of historical justice.

Famcare Inc. shared the story of treating a young woman who had escaped years of human trafficking, was living on the streets, and had no ID. This highlights the life-or-death situations where demanding documentation is not only impractical but inhumane.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Universal Opposition: The most striking pattern is the complete and total opposition to this outcome from every type of commenter—healthcare providers, educators, civil rights groups, local and state governments, and individuals. There is no constituency arguing in favor of this consequence.

'Crisis' as a Barrier: A recurring pattern is the observation that the need for safety-net services often coincides with a personal or community crisis (homelessness, natural disaster, domestic violence, health emergency)—the very moments when an individual is least likely to have their paperwork organized and accessible.

Rural vs. Urban Barriers: Commenters from rural areas frequently noted unique barriers for their populations, such as long travel distances to government offices, lack of reliable transportation, and poor internet access, which compound the difficulty of obtaining documents.

Program-Specific Harms: The nature of the harm is articulated differently by program type. Head Start commenters focus on the loss of irreplaceable developmental windows for young children. Healthcare commenters focus on immediate, life-threatening consequences of delayed or denied medical care.

keyQuotations.quote: [The policy will] place essential human services out of the reach of many vulnerable citizens and residents.
Many homeless Americans could be denied lifesaving care simply due to the absence of paperwork.
Imagine being required to bring a birth certificate to get care at a Community Health Center, or to provide a Social Security card to access mental health services.
During the time the family is in the program, it may take months to years for a family to get their child’s birth certificate with our assistance.
The biggest impact of the bill was denying or delaying services to applicants who were eligible but could not easily prove it.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Individual citing Colorado state law experience

keyQuotations.quote: ...many citizen individuals and families—including married women who have changed their name and African American elders who lack official birth documentation as a direct consequence of systemic discrimination during the Jim Crow era and beyond—may lack the credentials to verify their status...

Among the powerful strengths of the Community Health Center model is that we will give you access to vital, life saving care when you need it, even if you aren't able to show us your birth certificate.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Psychiatrist at a Community Health Center

keyQuotations.quote: In our community, it is difficult for families to obtain a birth certificate for their child due to the cost involved.
Do you really want US citizens to have to provide a birth certificate?
This administrative barrier will likely keep those who are citizens from applying as well, as many do not have passports and proof of citizenship readily available.
Additionally, about 30% of our children are being raised by grandparents or other friends and families, who may not have immediate access to these documents.

Had I not had a job that allowed me to do this... he easily could have left our house at age 18 without the documentation that would allow him to seek health care in this new interpretation of PRWORA.

Requiring a person experiencing a mental health or substance crisis to bring the right documentation... will result in programs turning away many people who are in fact citizens and 'qualified' immigrants.

sections.executiveSummary: Public input on this theme reflects a near-unanimous, vehement consensus that the proposed rule will inflict severe and disproportionate harm on a wide range of the nation's most vulnerable populations, irrespective of their immigration status. A massive form letter campaign underscores the widespread alarm. The central tension point identified by commenters is between the rule's proposed documentation and verification requirements and the lived realities of at-risk groups—including individuals experiencing homelessness, foster youth, rural residents, people with disabilities, and low-income families—who inherently lack stable access to such records. Commenters, particularly frontline service providers and advocacy groups, argue that these requirements will create insurmountable barriers, effectively denying U.S. citizens and other legally present individuals access to life-saving services. The overwhelming and dominant recommendation is to withdraw the reinterpretation entirely or, at a minimum, create broad exemptions for critical safety-net programs like Head Start and Community Health Centers to prevent the collapse of essential services.

consensusPoints.text: The rule will disproportionately harm the most vulnerable populations. Across 183 unique perspectives and a form letter campaign with over 6,150 submissions, there is absolute agreement that the rule will not impact all communities equally. Commenters argue it will create insurmountable barriers for groups already facing compounded socioeconomic challenges—such as individuals experiencing homelessness, foster youth, rural residents, people with disabilities, and low-income families—effectively punishing them for their circumstances.

exceptions.text: No commenters disputed this point. The consensus is absolute across all stakeholder types.

consensusPoints.text: Documentation requirements are unrealistic and create insurmountable barriers for at-risk groups. A strong majority of commenters, particularly direct service providers, state that requiring proof of citizenship is out of touch with the realities of marginalized populations. They argue that individuals experiencing homelessness, children in foster care, survivors of domestic violence, and families in deep poverty often lack stable addresses, funds, secure storage for, or immediate access to, essential records like birth certificates.

Rural communities face unique and compounded obstacles to access. A strong majority of commenters discussing geographic impacts highlighted that residents of rural and underserved areas will be disproportionately harmed. These communities already contend with limited services, lack of transportation, long travel distances to administrative offices, and poor internet access, all of which would be exacerbated by new documentation burdens, making the destabilization of a single service provider catastrophic for an entire region.

The rule will destabilize critical safety-net providers. Commenters from healthcare, early education, and social services unanimously agree that the rule threatens the operational and financial viability of essential community institutions like Head Start programs and Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs). They predict a ripple effect where harm to these institutions will damage entire communities, including the U.S. citizens who rely on their services.

areasOfDebate.topic: Impact of the Rule on Low-Income Citizens' Access to Services
areasOfDebate.description: The public input on this theme is characterized by an overwhelming consensus against the rule, with virtually no debate on its expected negative impacts. However, a single point of contrast was identified:

positions.label: Creates Insurmountable Barriers
positions.stance: The rule will harm low-income U.S. citizens and other legally present vulnerable groups by imposing documentation hurdles they cannot meet, leading to a net loss of essential services.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters (representing at least 182 of 183 unique perspectives).
keyArguments: Many vulnerable citizens (e.g., homeless, elderly, disabled, foster youth) lack easy access to birth certificates or state IDs due to cost, mobility issues, or life circumstances.
The "chilling effect" will deter eligible families from seeking help due to fear and confusion, even if they have the required documents.
Diverting resources to verification will strain service providers, reducing the quality and availability of services for everyone.

positions.label: Prioritizes Citizens
positions.stance: The rule could potentially benefit low-income citizens by prioritizing them for limited program slots, such as in Head Start.
positions.supportLevel: One individual commenter.
keyArguments: Some urban Head Start programs have waitlists, suggesting demand from citizens exceeds supply.
The rule could free up slots for "minority, BiPOC, and/or lower socioeconomic citizens" who are currently on these waitlists.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Systemic disenfranchisement and harm to marginalized communities; exacerbation of racial and economic disparities; violation of human rights and the missions of safety-net programs.
specificPoints: These groups provide detailed legal, policy, and data-driven analysis, connecting the rule to specific statutory programs (PATH, CSBG, SUBG) and broader patterns of social injustice. They frame the issue in terms of civil rights, equity, and the long-term societal costs of disinvestment.
Complete withdrawal of the reinterpretation; clear exemptions for all life/safety services.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Violation of their mission and federal mandate to serve all residents; creation of an impossible operational burden; erosion of patient trust; and devastating public health outcomes.
specificPoints: They offer a frontline view of the practical challenges and ethical dilemmas. They describe the reality of treating patients who arrive without documentation and predict increases in emergency room visits, the spread of untreated communicable diseases, and worsening chronic conditions.
An exemption for homeless health centers and the Health Center Program entirely; complete withdrawal of the rule.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Delayed or denied enrollment for the most at-risk children (homeless, foster care, fleeing violence), which they are mandated to prioritize; administrative burdens diverting resources from classrooms; and a breakdown of trust with families.
specificPoints: They stress the time-sensitive nature of early childhood development, arguing that even a few weeks of delay due to paperwork can have lasting negative consequences. They highlight the paradox that the rule creates the largest barriers for the very populations Head Start is required by law to serve.
Rescind the rule's application to Head Start; maintain the current eligibility framework.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Unfunded mandates; cost-shifting to local taxpayers; increased strain on local emergency services, shelters, and public health infrastructure; and conflict with existing state laws.
specificPoints: They quantify the local impact, translating a federal policy change into direct consequences for their budgets and services. They see the rule as an abdication of federal responsibility that will create crises for local governments to manage.
Withdrawal of the rule; federal funding to cover all associated administrative and service costs.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Individuals (with professional expertise or lived experience)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The direct, human cost of the policy on the people they serve or are a part of. This group includes social workers, physicians, nurses, educators, and former beneficiaries of programs.
specificPoints: Their comments provide powerful personal testimony, clinical observations, and anecdotes that illustrate the real-world consequences of restricting access to care.
Unanimously oppose the rule, often framing their plea in moral or ethical terms.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Historical Parallel to Deinstitutionalization: An individual with a background in public health administration warned that, just as the closure of psychiatric facilities led to a crisis of homelessness among people with serious mental illness, this rule could have similar unintended consequences by cutting off community-based support systems.

Documentation as a Tool of Disenfranchisement: One commenter drew a powerful connection between the rule's documentation requirements and laws designed to suppress voting among vulnerable populations, suggesting this is part of a broader pattern of using bureaucratic barriers to exclude marginalized groups.

Foster Youth are Uniquely Trapped: John Burton Advocates for Youth and the Youth Law Center point out the profound injustice for foster youth, stating, "This small, but vulnerable group of students did not choose to immigrate to the United States, nor did they choose to be the victim of abuse and neglect..." The rule would punish them for circumstances entirely beyond their control.

Foster Youth are Uniquely Trapped: John Burton Advocates for Youth and the Youth Law Center point out the profound injustice for foster youth, stating, "This small, but vulnerable group of students did not choose to immigrate to the United States, nor did they choose to be the victim of abuse and neglect..." The rule would punish them for circumstances entirely beyond their control.

Rural Disadvantage is Acute: First Focus on Children provides data showing 46% of all Head Start slots are in rural congressional districts. CRLA Foundation et al. note Head Start is often the sole service provider for families in remote communities, where child poverty is 37% higher than in metro areas.

Rural Disadvantage is Acute: First Focus on Children provides data showing 46% of all Head Start slots are in rural congressional districts. CRLA Foundation et al. note Head Start is often the sole service provider for families in remote communities, where child poverty is 37% higher than in metro areas.

Interconnected Vulnerability: The American Association of People with Disabilities points out that harming immigrant caregivers by denying them services will have a direct, negative impact on the U.S. citizens with disabilities and older adults who depend on their care.

Data-backed Observation on Broader Impact: Several health providers cite HRSA data that in 2024, CHCs served over 428,000 veterans and 4.2 million seniors, arguing that destabilizing these centers harms a much broader population than just immigrants.

A Court Appointed Special Advocate (CASA) for foster youth poignantly states that for this population, "nutrition, health, education and employment services are often the difference between life and death".

A parent shared a personal story of how Early Head Start was a critical support that allowed her to re-enter the workforce while her family was struggling, illustrating the direct link between these programs and family economic stability.

Unique Vulnerability of Pacific Islanders: The Pacific Islands Primary Care Association provided a unique perspective, explaining that CHCs in the U.S.-Affiliated Pacific Islands have a different payer mix and serve populations with significant health disparities. The closure of these trusted centers would be devastating.

The Paradox of Wealth and Need: Community Action Marin observes, "Marin is one of the wealthiest counties in the nation, and yet too many in our community struggle to make ends meet," showing that safety-net programs are critical everywhere.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: Commenters consistently identified rural communities and the South as uniquely disadvantaged. Rural areas are seen as fragile due to distance, lack of transportation, and a lack of alternative service providers. The South is highlighted for its higher poverty rates and greater reliance on programs like Head Start. Urban commenters focus more on the sheer scale of impact on shelter systems and services for dense populations.

Unintended Consequences / Cascade of Harm: A clear pattern emerged where commenters predicted a cascade of negative knock-on effects. The most common chain reaction identified was: denying primary/preventative care -> shifting costs to expensive emergency rooms -> creating financial instability for hospitals (especially rural ones) -> leading to closures and creating a "crisis of care" for the entire community.

Erosion of Trust: Multiple commenters, especially those serving homeless populations and Head Start families, noted that forcing providers to act as immigration enforcement will shatter decades of trust built with marginalized communities, causing people to avoid seeking help even when eligible.

Intersectionality of Vulnerability: Commenters frequently described how vulnerabilities intersect and compound each other—a low-income family in a rural area with a child with a disability, for example, would face multiple, overlapping barriers created by this single rule.

Focus on Specific Programs as Lifelines: Commenters consistently identified a handful of programs as absolutely essential for specific vulnerable populations: Head Start for low-income children and those with disabilities; Community Health Centers (FQHCs) for rural residents and the uninsured; and the PATH program as the last line of defense for individuals with serious mental illness experiencing homelessness.

keyQuotations.quote: [The rule will] weaken the infrastructure meant to support the most vulnerable people of our country, not just immigrants.

American citizens who do not have documents on hand at any given moment to prove their citizenship status will be denied services too. This is highly common for poor, homeless, seniors and rural Americans.

The instability our families endure often makes it difficult, if not impossible, to obtain and retain documentation. Re-obtaining documentation requires the expenditure of time and money these families simply do not have.
In rural counties like ours, where resources are scarce and nonprofits often fill critical service gaps, this change in federal policy will create a cascade of harm.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Executive Director, Del Norte Child Care Council

keyQuotations.quote: This small, but vulnerable group of students [foster youth] did not choose to immigrate to the United States, nor did they choose to be the victim of abuse and neglect...
The policy makes Head Start’s responsiveness virtually impossible, replacing a family-centered approach with an inflexible, legalistic barrier.
[I] have seen firsthand how nutrition, health, education and employment services are often the difference between life and death for vulnerable populations.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Volunteer Court Appointed Special Advocate for foster youth

keyQuotations.quote: People who are experiencing homelessness are extremely distrustful of systems and institutions that they feel have failed them in the past. This requirement could result in a loss of trust that will disrupt decades of work done by health care for the homeless providers.
It will drive the disenfranchised into local emergency rooms, a very cost-effective means of addressing primary care and behavioral health needs.
Households that include autistic individuals face a double burden from administrative barriers, as the households of autistic individuals tend to be disproportionately lower-income...
I live in the state of Arizona; homelessness and starvation are equivalent to a death sentence there.

The closure of CHCs does not just affect immigrant patients; it affects entire communities, including low-income citizens, veterans, people with disabilities, and others who rely on the CHC for accessible, affordable care.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Asian Human Services Family Health Center

keyQuotations.quote: Are you not abandoning our children without calculating the moral and long term cost of an unjust government action that only causes harm to the weakest members of society and humanity.
Poor children are poor. Period.
Stop attacking the poorest among us.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on this theme is overwhelmingly unified in its opposition to any policy change that would restrict access to services for specific non-citizen groups. A vast and diverse coalition of stakeholders—including healthcare providers, advocacy groups, government entities, and individuals—expresses profound concern that the reinterpretation harms not just undocumented immigrants but a wide array of lawfully present individuals, such as DACA recipients, TPS holders, and various visa holders. The central tension is between this near-unanimous call for protecting and expanding access versus a single commenter's call for strict adherence to existing law to ensure fiscal responsibility. The dominant recommendation is to reject the proposed reinterpretation and, in many cases, to expand the definition of "qualified immigrant" to include more lawfully present groups.

consensusPoints.text: The policy harms a wide range of lawfully present non-citizens.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (at least 140 of 153 perspectives)

exceptions.text: One commenter (CPAC Foundation) does not dispute that these groups would be excluded but views this exclusion as a correct and necessary enforcement of existing law.

consensusPoints.text: Existing PRWORA law is already restrictive and excludes many lawfully present groups.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, particularly advocacy groups and legal experts (over 60)

exceptions.text: No commenters disputed this characterization of PRWORA.

consensusPoints.text: DACA and TPS holders are particularly vulnerable and deserving of protection.

exceptions.text: None.

areasOfDebate.description: The commentary reflects near-universal opposition to the proposed reinterpretation, creating a stark contrast with one dissenting viewpoint.

positions.label: Broad Access
positions.stance: The reinterpretation is a harmful overreach; access to essential services should be maintained or expanded for all residents, especially those lawfully present.
positions.supportLevel: Held by nearly all commenters (representing 152 of 153 unique perspectives), including a broad coalition of healthcare providers, advocacy groups, government entities, labor unions, and individuals.
keyArguments: Public Health Imperative: Denying preventative care to any group increases community-wide health risks and shifts costs to overburdened emergency rooms.
Economic Contributions: Immigrants in statuses like DACA, TPS, and H-2A are vital to the economy, work in essential sectors, and pay taxes, earning the right to access services.
Moral & Ethical Duty: It is unethical for providers to abandon patients and cruel for society to deny care to vulnerable people, especially children and survivors of violence.

positions.label: Strict Adherence
positions.stance: The reinterpretation correctly enforces the letter and intent of PRWORA, which was designed to limit non-citizen access to public benefits.
positions.supportLevel: One organization.
keyArguments: Upholding Congressional Intent: PRWORA explicitly made most non-citizens ineligible for benefits to promote self-sufficiency and protect taxpayer funds.
Fiscal Responsibility: Enforcing the five-year bar for qualified aliens and eligibility rules for others is a necessary and transparent use of public money.
Preventing Abuse: Strict adherence is needed to monitor for potential abuses or expansions of eligibility, particularly regarding refugee and asylum designations.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Harm to a wide spectrum of lawfully present individuals (DACA, TPS, U/T visa holders, SIJS); exacerbation of existing inequities under PRWORA; chilling effects on eligible families.
specificPoints: These groups provide the most detailed legal and historical analysis of PRWORA, often listing the precise categories of "qualified" vs. "unqualified" immigrants to demonstrate the breadth of who is excluded. They highlight the impact on highly vulnerable but legally present subgroups like survivors of trafficking and violence.
Withdraw the reinterpretation; expand the definition of "qualified immigrant"; pass legislation to provide a pathway to citizenship.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ethical conflicts (patient abandonment); negative public health outcomes (disease spread); operational chaos and financial instability for health centers; cost-shifting to emergency departments.
specificPoints: They translate policy into on-the-ground reality, describing the faces of patients they will have to turn away (DACA recipients, TPS holders, H-2A workers) and the direct consequences for community health. They emphasize their legal mandate as FQHCs to serve all residents.
Rescind the policy to allow them to continue providing care to all residents regardless of status.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Contradiction of legislative intent; administrative burdens and confusion for state and local agencies; unfunded mandates and cost-shifting to local governments.
specificPoints: These stakeholders speak with institutional authority, with Members of Congress referencing legislative history and state agencies detailing the direct conflict with their own program rules and the operational impossibility of implementation.
Withdraw the reinterpretation.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Injustice and cruelty of the policy; harm to people they know personally; unfairness of targeting people who pay taxes and contribute to society.
specificPoints: Individuals provide powerful, emotionally resonant testimony and personal stories that humanize the abstract policy debate. They speak from direct experience as DACA recipients, spouses of DACA recipients, or friends of immigrants.
Reconsider the policy for DACA residents; grant a pathway to amnesty; treat people with compassion.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Adara Newsome provides a unique and critical insight that broad regulatory changes often fail to account for the unique legal status of COFA citizens, leading to their prolonged and unintended exclusion from essential programs. This serves as a powerful warning about the need for precision in regulatory language.

The Southern Education Foundation and Start Early point out that the Regulatory Impact Analysis's conflation of these terms is problematic. Many lawfully present individuals (like DACA/TPS holders) are "unauthorized" for benefits under PRWORA, and this imprecise language exacerbates uncertainty for program implementation.

Kayla Sweeney offers a compelling personal example of her DACA recipient husband being legally required to pay taxes while being barred from receiving benefits. Her framing—"We exploit DACA recipients for free tax revenue"—presents a sharp moral and economic argument against the policy.

An unexpected angle from Todd Bailey highlights that community health centers themselves may employ DACA recipients, putting the centers in a potentially discriminatory environment and showing how the rule harms these individuals as both patients and employees.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A Broad and Unified Coalition: Opposition to the rule is not limited to immigration groups. It includes a remarkably diverse coalition of healthcare providers, child welfare advocates, disability rights organizations, labor unions, faith-based groups, and local governments, indicating the issue's far-reaching impact.

Linkage to Economic Sectors: A clear pattern emerged connecting immigration policy to the stability of key economic sectors. The harm to H-2A visa holders was consistently linked to the agricultural food supply, while the exclusion of DACA recipients was often tied to the healthcare workforce shortage.

Legal vs. On-the-Ground Framing: A pattern in argumentation is visible. Legal and policy-focused groups (e.g., ACLU, NILC) build their case around the statutory history and language of PRWORA. In contrast, direct service providers (e.g., FQHCs, Head Start programs) frame their arguments around the immediate, practical, and ethical consequences for the people they serve daily.

Geographic Variations in Focus: While the rule is federal, commenters often highlighted region-specific impacts: the unique status of COFA citizens in Arkansas and the Pacific Islands, the reliance on H-2A workers in agricultural states like North Carolina and Georgia, and the high concentration of diverse immigrant populations in California.

keyQuotations.quote: Restricting immigrant access to these services, even for those here legally but not classified as “qualified aliens,” risks accelerating health disparities, threatening family stability, and increasing public health costs long term.
The policy will cause 'great harm to many categories of immigrants, refugees and asylees, including those who are here lawfully'.
Thus, these restrictions harm not only undocumented immigrants, but also some lawfully present individuals.
In short, denying services to LEGAL immigrants will cause economic and health harms to the wider communities in which they live and work.
Finally, including DACA residents in the 'non-qualified' group is short-sighted and nonsensical.

My husband, as a DACA recipient, was barred from accessing all the benefits that undocumented peoples are being barred from accessing now, but still was lawfully responsible for paying taxes to our state and federal government.
...it is critical that any new rules are written with intentional inclusion of COFA immigrants to ensure they are not once again left without access to vital services.

I especially am concerned about the unprecedented inclusion of H2A legal guest workers under this prohibition. These farmworkers are here with a temporary legal work visa and deserve access to healthcare while working on our farms and supporting our agricultural economy.
Providers could be forced to discontinue care for established legal immigrant patients, risking patient abandonment and ethical issues.
As medical students and trainees, many of us complete rotations in FQHCs and see several undocumented, DACA, or TPS patients in a day.
it took over a decade for Marshallese residents in Arkansas to gain access to Medicaid due to regulatory language that did not properly include them.

[We urge HHS to] ensure strict adherence to these eligibility criteria and carefully monitor for any potential abuses or expansions beyond the congressional intent, particularly regarding refugee and asylum designations.

This excluded some people who are lawfully present, including individuals with Temporary Protected Status, people with nonimmigrant visas, and individuals granted deferred action, including Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA).

It also includes other lawfully present survivors, including those granted U nonimmigrant status, those with Special Immigrant Juvenile status, survivors with Temporary Protected Status or DACA, asylum applicants, and survivors with legal status as nonimmigrant visa holders, among others.
